---
title: "LLM-assisted projects: practical tips"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
---

{{< include /_includes/hover-popups.qmd >}}

A short set of working principles I've converged on after a stretch of building R packages, dashboards, and analyses with Claude Code as a daily collaborator. None of these are novel — they're cribbed and consolidated from people much further along — but they're the ones that actually changed how I work.

## Three load-bearing principles

::: {.callout-important}
Before you read further: LLM-assisted development only works if you bake in **reproducibility**, **consistency**, and **validation** from day one. These are not optional extras to add later.

- **Reproducibility:** Every output is regenerated from locked source (git + Nix/pip lockfiles). No hand-tweaked CSV exports, no manual edits to deployed assets.
- **Consistency:** Code style, naming, structure, and test style are enforced mechanically (linters, formatters, pre-commit hooks). An LLM builds to a spec faster when the spec is non-negotiable.
- **Validation:** Every claim is testable. Tests drive the spec; the spec drives the code. If you can't write a test for it, you don't ship it.

The rest of this site and the [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/JohnGavin/llm/blob/main/AGENTS.md) file at the repo root detail these practices. Start there if you're new to this workflow.
:::

## The shape of the day

**Plan more than you execute. 60% planning, 40% execution.**

Counter-intuitive at first because LLMs are so fast you assume more execution = more output. In practice the opposite holds: an LLM can implement a clear spec in minutes but struggles for hours against a fuzzy one. Time spent sharpening the spec pays back several times over, because the fast part stays fast and the slow part (rework, blind alleys, conflicting context) shrinks.

The pattern that works for me:

1. **Sketch what you want.** WHAT, not HOW. Success criteria, not step-by-step instructions.
2. **Hand the sketch to a fresh LLM and ask it to tear the spec apart.** Identify points that are underspecified, inconsistent, or weird. Iterate until the remaining questions are genuinely niche.
3. **Hand the sharpened plan to a new LLM with a simple prompt.** "Implement this." If you've done step 2 well, the simple prompt is enough.

The rationale for separating step 2 from step 3 is that the LLM doing the critique should not be the one doing the build — different framings, different blind spots.

## Declarative beats imperative

Tell the LLM what success looks like, not which buttons to press.

- **Imperative:** "Read file X, parse the JSON, filter to dates after Y, write to Z."
- **Declarative:** "Produce a CSV at Z containing only rows from X where date > Y. Tests should cover empty input, malformed JSON, and the date boundary."

Declarative prompts let the LLM bring its training data to bear. Imperative prompts pin it to your particular sequence and waste that.

This is also why **writing tests first** is so productive in this workflow: tests are declarative by nature. They state success, not procedure. If you can write the test, the LLM can write the implementation.

## Parallel execution

Multiple models, multiple worktrees, in parallel:

- A cheap fast model (Haiku) handles mechanical edits — typos, renames, version bumps.
- A mid-tier model (Sonnet) handles per-file work — debugging, single-feature implementation.
- A larger model (Opus) handles synthesis, architecture, and conversation with you.

Use `git worktree add` so two sessions can work on independent branches without stepping on each other. The orchestrator session decides what to delegate; the worker sessions just do the work.

The cost gradient matters more than people expect. Doing all your work at the top tier when most of it is mechanical is the single biggest budget leak.

## What you lose

Worth being honest about:

- **You lose the ability to write code from a cold start.** The muscle atrophies if you don't exercise it. I now find myself reaching for the LLM for things I could have typed in 30 seconds, and the cumulative effect on craft is real.
- **You ship code you have not read.** Even with diff review, the volume eventually exceeds what you can scan attentively. The remedy is mechanical: tests, types, code review, and a willingness to revert. The remedy is not "read every diff"; that's a stage you grow out of.

Neither is a reason not to do this work. They're costs to acknowledge so you don't pretend they don't exist.

## What's covered elsewhere

The three principles above are elaborated in detail across the project:

- **Reproducibility:** Nix + rix workflows, lockfile discipline, package versioning (see `nix-rix-r-environment` skill and `default.nix` patterns)
- **Consistency:** Code style guides, linters, and pre-commit hooks (see `tidyverse-style`, `accessibility` rules, and `.claude/scripts/r_code_check.sh`)
- **Validation:** Test-driven development, quality gates, and analytical review checklists (see `test-driven-development`, `quality-gates` skills and `analytical-review-checklist` rule)

## Dynamic Target Generation with targets

When building complex data pipelines with `{targets}`, you need to understand how target expressions work and when you might need programmatic target generation.

### How tar_target() Works

The `tar_target()` function uses **non-standard evaluation (NSE)** — it captures expressions without evaluating them immediately:

```r
# Standard usage: expression is captured, not evaluated
tar_target(name, mean(c(1, 2, 3)))
```

This is NOT string evaluation. The expression `mean(c(1, 2, 3))` is captured as an R expression object and evaluated later by the targets framework.

**Common misconception:** Passing a string to `tar_target()` does NOT execute that string as code:

```r
# This creates a target with value "1+1" (the string), not 2
tar_target(wrong, "1+1")
```

### When You Need Programmatic Generation

Sometimes you need to build targets dynamically — generating target names or expressions programmatically. For this, use `tar_target_raw()`:

```r
# Generate targets programmatically
target_names <- c("summary_a", "summary_b", "summary_c")
target_list <- lapply(target_names, function(nm) {
  tar_target_raw(
    nm,
    quote(summarise_data(data)),
    deps = "data"
  )
})
```

The key difference: `tar_target_raw()` expects:
- Target name as a **string**
- Command as a **quoted expression** (via `quote()`)
- Dependencies as a **character vector**

### Variable Capture in Dynamic Targets

When generating targets programmatically, be careful with variable scoping:

```r
threshold <- 100

# WRONG: 'threshold' refers to the variable, not the value
tar_target(filtered, filter(data, value > threshold))
# If threshold changes later, target invalidates

# RIGHT: Use substitute() to capture the value
tar_target_raw(
  "filtered",
  substitute(filter(data, value > x), list(x = threshold))
)
```

### When to Use Each Approach

| Use tar_target() when | Use tar_target_raw() when |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Writing targets by hand | Generating targets in a loop |
| Target names are known at write time | Target names come from data/config |
| Expressions are static | Expressions need programmatic assembly |
| You want IDE support and refactoring | Code generation is unavoidable |

**Default to tar_target().** Programmatic generation adds complexity — use it only when the alternative is repetitive manual code.

### Debugging Challenges with Dynamic Targets

Programmatically generated targets are harder to debug:

1. The target definition is built at runtime, not visible in `_targets.R`
2. IDE tools (go-to-definition, refactoring) don't work
3. Errors in `substitute()` or `quote()` produce cryptic messages

When a dynamic target fails:
1. Use `tar_manifest()` to see the generated target definition
2. Extract the expression and test it interactively
3. Consider whether the complexity is justified

### Readability Wins

Even when you CAN generate targets programmatically, ask whether you SHOULD:

```r
# Programmatic but hard to scan
datasets <- c("sales", "inventory", "customers")
lapply(datasets, function(d) {
  tar_target_raw(
    paste0("summary_", d),
    substitute(summarise_data(dat), list(dat = as.name(d)))
  )
})

# Explicit and clear
list(
  tar_target(summary_sales, summarise_data(sales)),
  tar_target(summary_inventory, summarise_data(inventory)),
  tar_target(summary_customers, summarise_data(customers))
)
```

If you have three targets, write them out. Save the machinery for when you have dozens.

### Examples from This Project

The `llm` package's pipelines (see `R/tar_plans/plan_*.R`) primarily use explicit `tar_target()` calls rather than programmatic generation. This is intentional — the pipelines are small enough that readability beats abstraction.

When dynamic generation is needed (e.g., in external projects with many similar targets), the pattern is:
1. Generate with `tar_target_raw()` and `substitute()`
2. Validate with `tar_manifest()` before committing
3. Document why generation is needed (repetition? config-driven?)

### Related

- `targets` manual on metaprogramming: <https://books.ropensci.org/targets/dynamic.html>
- `targets` documentation: <https://books.ropensci.org/targets/>

## TODOs

- Broaden the framing beyond R. The principles apply identically to TypeScript, Rust, Go, and C++ projects — the only thing that changes is which test framework you reach for first. Add explicit examples from a non-R project once one matures here.

```{r build-info}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
sha <- tryCatch(substr(system("git rev-parse HEAD", intern = TRUE), 1, 7),
                error = function(e) "N/A")
cat(sprintf(
  "---\n\nllm | [Git %s](https://github.com/JohnGavin/llm/commit/%s) | R %s | Built %s\n",
  sha, sha, paste(R.version$major, R.version$minor, sep = "."),
  format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
))
```

## Methodology

### What this vignette computes

This vignette presents practical principles for LLM-assisted development — spec-first planning, declarative prompting, multi-model delegation, and targets pipeline patterns — drawn from experience building R packages with Claude Code. It also provides a reference guide to `tar_target()` vs `tar_target_raw()` with scoping examples. Content is static prose and code examples; no targets pipeline data is read at render time.

### Data sources

- Build info: `git rev-parse HEAD` (current commit SHA), `R.version` (R version), `Sys.time()` (render timestamp) — computed inline at render time, not from the targets pipeline

### AI disclosure

This vignette was developed with assistance from Anthropic's Claude (model: Opus 4.7 and Sonnet 4.6). AI helped with code structure, prose drafting, and visualization choices. All analytical decisions and data interpretations are the author's responsibility.

{{< include ../../_includes/qr-footer.qmd >}}
